title: Principled Negotiation (Getting to Yes)
simple_html:
Principled negotiation is a method for reaching agreements that are wise, efficient, and good for the relationship — instead of treating the other side as an enemy you must defeat. It comes from the book Getting to Yes by Roger Fisher and William Ury of the Harvard Negotiation Project.

The core idea: stop arguing over positions (the specific things each side demands) and start exploring interests (the underlying needs and reasons behind those demands). Two people can fight over the last orange — but if one wants the peel for baking and the other wants the juice, both can get 100% of what they need.

The four principles

  • Separate the people from the problem. Be soft on the people, hard on the problem. Don't let emotions and personalities turn into the fight itself.
  • Focus on interests, not positions. Ask "why?" and "why not?" to find what each side really needs.
  • Invent options for mutual gain. Brainstorm many possible solutions before deciding — grow the pie before slicing it.
  • Insist on objective criteria. Settle using fair standards (market price, expert opinion, law) rather than a contest of wills.

Key takeaways

  • Positions are what people say they want; interests are why they want it.
  • Most conflicts have shared or compatible interests hiding behind clashing positions.
  • Fair, external standards protect the relationship and make agreements stick.
  • The goal is a wise agreement reached efficiently and amicably — not "winning."

Principled negotiation is sometimes called "negotiation on the merits." It is the foundation for nearly every modern win-win negotiation course.
intermediate_html:
Principled negotiation — also called "negotiation on the merits" — was developed by Roger Fisher and William Ury in Getting to Yes (1981) at the Harvard Negotiation Project. It offers a middle path between two flawed styles: the soft negotiator who concedes to keep peace (and gets exploited) and the hard negotiator who treats every deal as a contest of will (and damages relationships and outcomes). The merits-based approach is hard on the problem and soft on the people.

The four pillars

1. Separate the people from the problem. Negotiators are human: they have egos, emotions, and a need to save face. When relationship issues get tangled with substance, attacks feel personal. Address perception (try to see it their way), emotion (acknowledge and let it out), and communication (listen actively, speak about yourself not about them). Sit side-by-side facing the problem, not across the table facing each other.

2. Focus on interests, not positions. A position is a stated demand ("I want $60,000"). An interest is the underlying need (security, recognition, fairness, fear of being underpaid relative to peers). Behind opposed positions lie many more interests than conflicting ones — and some are simply different, which creates room to trade. Probe with "Why?" and "Why not?"

3. Invent options for mutual gain. Premature judgment, searching for a single answer, the assumption of a fixed pie, and the belief that "their problem is their problem" all block creativity. Separate inventing from deciding: brainstorm widely first, then evaluate. Look for shared interests and dovetail differing interests (in risk, time, forecasts) into joint gains.

4. Insist on objective criteria. When interests directly conflict, don't let it become a stubbornness contest. Agree on fair standards — market value, precedent, professional standards, what a court would decide, cost — and fair procedures (one cuts, the other chooses). Frame each issue as a joint search for the fair answer; reason and be open to reason, but never yield to pressure.

Worked mini-case

A manager (M) and an employee (E) negotiate a raise. Positions: E demands $70k; M offers $58k — a $12k gap and rising tension. Switching to interests: E reveals the real need is recognition and parity with a peer who earns more; M's real concern is the salary-band budget and setting a precedent. Options for mutual gain: a $62k base plus a title change (recognition), a one-time bonus (avoids permanently raising the band), and a six-month review tied to metrics. Objective criteria: they pull market salary data for the role and the company's published pay bands to justify the number. Result: a wise agreement both can defend, reached without a relationship-damaging standoff.

How to apply it

  • Before negotiating, list your interests and hypothesize theirs, ranked by importance.
  • Open by exploring interests and acknowledging emotions before discussing numbers.
  • Run a no-commitment brainstorm to generate multiple packaged options.
  • Anchor the final terms to a fair, external standard so both sides can say yes without losing face.

Principled negotiation pairs naturally with knowing your BATNA (your walk-away alternative), covered in the next lesson — interests tell you what to pursue, BATNA tells you when to walk.
full_html:
Principled negotiation, or "negotiation on the merits," is the framework articulated by Roger Fisher and William Ury in Getting to Yes: Negotiating Agreement Without Giving In (Fisher & Ury, 1981; 3rd ed. with Bruce Patton, 2011), the flagship product of the Harvard Negotiation Project. It reframes negotiation away from a zero-sum positional contest and toward a joint problem-solving exercise that aims for three simultaneous goals: a wise agreement (one that meets legitimate interests, is durable, and considers community interests), efficiency, and the preservation — ideally improvement — of the relationship between the parties.

The problem with positional bargaining

Fisher and Ury argue that haggling over positions is inefficient and dangerous. It produces unwise outcomes because parties lock into stated demands and defend egos rather than needs; it is inefficient because of the slow dance of extreme opening offers, small concessions, and brinkmanship; and it endangers relationships as it becomes a contest of will. Crucially, choosing between a soft and a hard positional style is a false choice: the soft player makes concessions to preserve the relationship and is exploited by a hard player, while two hard players reach impasse or a bitter, inefficient deal. Principled negotiation transcends the soft/hard axis with a third game played on the merits.

The four prescriptions

People: Separate the people from the problem. Every negotiator has two kinds of interest — in the substance and in the relationship — and the two easily become entangled because positional bargaining puts relationship and substance into direct conflict. Fisher and Ury decompose "people problems" into three categories: perception (put yourself in their shoes, don't deduce intentions from your own fears, don't blame, make proposals consistent with their values, give them a stake in the outcome to save face), emotion (recognize and make emotions explicit, allow the other side to let off steam without reacting, use symbolic gestures), and communication (listen actively and acknowledge what is said, speak to be understood, speak about yourself — "I feel let down" — not about them, speak for a purpose). The maxim is to be soft on the people, hard on the problem.

Interests: Focus on interests, not positions. The orange parable — two sisters split an orange in half, when one wanted only the peel for baking and the other only the fruit to eat — illustrates that positions obscure interests and that satisfying interests can expand value (Fisher & Ury, 1981). Behind opposed positions lie shared, compatible, and merely different interests. The most powerful interests are basic human needs: security, economic well-being, a sense of belonging, recognition, and control over one's life (drawing on Maslow, 1943). Practical moves include asking "Why?" and "Why not?", making your interests vivid and legitimate, and being concrete yet flexible.

Options: Invent options for mutual gain. Four obstacles inhibit option generation: premature judgment, searching for the single answer, the assumption of a fixed pie, and thinking that "solving their problem is their problem." The remedies are to separate inventing from deciding (e.g., a brainstorming session with criticism deferred), broaden the options using a tool like the Circle Chart (move between the specific and the general — problem, analysis, approaches, action ideas), search for mutual gain by dovetailing differing interests, and make their decision easy. This is the integrative engine of the method and connects to the broader literature on creating value (Lax & Sebenius, 1986; Walton & McKersie, 1965).

Criteria: Insist on using objective criteria. Where interests are directly opposed, the parties should commit to settling on the basis of fair standards (market value, precedent, scientific judgment, professional standards, cost, efficiency, what a court would decide) and fair procedures (the "I cut, you choose" principle; taking turns; drawing lots; using a third party). The behavioral rule is to frame each issue as a joint search for objective criteria, reason and remain open to reason as to which standards apply, and never yield to pressure — only to principle.

The fifth element: BATNA

Although the four prescriptions are the headline, Getting to Yes adds a critical fifth idea: protect yourself by knowing your BATNA — Best Alternative To a Negotiated Agreement. The BATNA is the standard against which any proposed deal should be measured; it is the true source of power in a negotiation and the antidote to over-eagerness to agree (developed fully in the BATNA / Reservation Price / ZOPA lesson, no. 7). A related response to coercive tactics is "negotiation jujitsu" — refusing to react to attacks, deflecting them onto the problem, and using the one-text procedure or the help of a third party.

Worked example

Consider a tenant and landlord renewing a lease. Positional version: tenant demands no rent increase; landlord demands +15%; they argue, the relationship sours, and they split the difference at +7.5% with both resentful. Principled version: they separate people from problem (the landlord stops treating late-night noise complaints as personal). Interests surface — the tenant wants predictable, affordable cost and a longer commitment; the landlord wants reliable occupancy, on-time payment, and protection against inflation. Options are invented: a two-year lease (occupancy security) with a modest year-one increase tied to a published rent index and a year-two adjustment capped at CPI (objective criteria), plus a small reduction in exchange for the tenant handling minor maintenance. The result satisfies more interests for both than the positional compromise and is anchored to standards neither party controls.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Treating "win-win" as soft. Principled negotiation is not capitulation; it is firm on interests and standards while gentle with people.
  • Confusing positions with interests. "$70,000" is a position; "I need parity with my peer" is an interest. Negotiators who never ask "why" stay trapped in positional combat.
  • Skipping the invention phase. Jumping straight to evaluation kills creative, value-creating options.
  • Ignoring the relationship cost. A deal won by domination can poison future dealings; the method explicitly values the ongoing relationship.
  • Forgetting the BATNA. Without knowing your alternative you cannot tell a good agreement from a bad one, and you negotiate from fear.

Debates and nuances

Critics note that the method can be naive against a purely adversarial, dishonest counterpart, and that interest-based approaches assume good-faith disclosure that competitive bargainers will exploit — the "negotiator's dilemma" of simultaneously creating and claiming value (Lax & Sebenius, 1986). Ury's later work Getting Past No (1991) and the second edition's chapter "What if they won't play?" address dealing with hard bargainers; Getting to Yes also acknowledges power realities through BATNA. Subsequent scholarship (Thompson, 2015; Malhotra & Bazerman, 2007) integrates principled negotiation with behavioral findings on anchoring, framing, and cognitive bias. Cross-culturally, the emphasis on explicit interest articulation and individual problem-solving reflects a Western, low-context orientation that must be adapted in high-context cultures.

Cross-links

This lesson builds on the distinction between distributive and integrative negotiation (no. 5) — principled negotiation is the practical operating system for integrative bargaining. It is completed by BATNA, Reservation Price & ZOPA (no. 7), which supplies the quantitative backbone, and by Tactics, Biases, Multiparty & Ethics (no. 8), which prepares you to apply the method when the other side plays hardball.
